meta.title: F07 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: BMW 5 Series F07 engine replacement costs — N47, N57, N55, N63 petrol and diesel. Real UK prices, reliability data, V8 ownership costs, and fastback‑specific guidance from 20+ vetted BMW specialists.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 5 Series F07 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F07 engine typically costs £3,200–£10,000 supply only, depending on the engine — the N47 in the 520d sits at £3,200–£5,500, the N57 in the 530d/535d is £4,500–£8,000, and the N63 in the 550i is £6,000–£10,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 5 Series F07 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N57D30A in the pre-LCI 530d (2009–2012) is the most dependable engine in the F07 range — the 3.0-litre straight-six diesel is chain-driven, with a robust bottom end and proven reliability across the BMW range. The N55B30A in the 535i is the most reliable petrol option. The N63 V8 in the 550i is the least reliable and most expensive to maintain.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 5 Series F07?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain stretch on the N47 in the 520d, valve stem seal failure on the N63 in the 550i, and turbocharger wear on the N57S in the 535d are the main engine issues. Air suspension compressor failure is the most common non-engine issue. The F07's unique fastback body doesn't affect engine reliability but can make some repairs more complex.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 5 Series F07 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The N57 diesel sixes and N55 petrol six are well-regarded and proven. The N47 diesel four carries a timing-chain risk — budget for inspection or replacement. The N63 V8 is the least reliable and most expensive to maintain — its hot-vee design causes heat-related failures. As with any BMW, service history is everything.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 5 Series F07 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: We don't say 'avoid' outright — but an N47-powered 520d without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one we consistently advise against. An N63-powered 550i without valve stem seal history is also a significant gamble. A well-maintained N57 530d or N55 535i is the safest choice.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F07 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (N47), blue smoke on startup (N63 valve stem seals), loss of power or turbo whine (N57S), or check engine lights are the key warning signs. On the 550i, excessive oil consumption is the classic N63 symptom. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 5 Series F07 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £15,000 F07, a £4,500–£7,000 N57 recon is 30–47% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £8,000 example, the same recon is 56–87% — that crosses our 70% BMW threshold, so we'd advise walking away. For an N63 550i, the economics are even less favourable unless the car is low-mileage and well-specified.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 5 Series F07 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N57 diesel six can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing — it's one of BMW's most durable engines. The N55 petrol six is similarly robust. The N47's limiting factor is the timing chain — addressed proactively, it can reach 150,000+ miles. The N63 V8 is the least durable — valve stem seal failure is common before 100,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F07, F10, and F11?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F10 is the 5 Series saloon (2009–2017), the F11 is the 5 Series Touring estate (2009–2017), and the F07 is the 5 Series Gran Turismo (2009–2017). All three share the same platform and engine range. The F07 is the fastback variant with a larger body, more rear legroom, and a two-piece tailgate. This page covers the F07 specifically — see the F10 and F11 pages for saloon and touring variants.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 5 Series F07 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. Common F07 codes include N47D20C/N47N (520d), N57D30A (530d pre-LCI), N57D30B (530d LCI), N57S (535d), N55B30A (535i), and N63B44/N63B44B (550i). Tell us your variant and registration date (pre- or post-2013 LCI) and we'll identify it for you.

hero.h1: BMW 5 Series F07 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the 5 Series Gran Turismo F07 — from the 2.0-litre N47 diesel that defines the entry-level 520d, to the 3.0-litre N57 six-cylinder diesel in the 530d and 535d, and the twin-turbo V8 N63 that powers the 550i. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the 2013 facelift that brought updated engines to BMW's most distinctive 5 Series. Backed by 300+ real UK F10/F07 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 5 Series F07 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F07 Gran Turismo — from the entry-level 520d to the range-topping 550i, and the 2013 facelift engine updates.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: The F07 uses BMW's established engine families — N47 (four-cylinder diesel), N57 (six-cylinder diesel), N55 (six-cylinder petrol), and N63 (V8 petrol). The N57 is widely regarded as the most dependable diesel in this range. The N55 replaced the N54 and was introduced in the F07. The N63 V8 carries the highest risk due to its hot-vee design. All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] pending dedicated Engine Pages and verified enquiry data for this specific model.

overview.intro: The BMW 5 Series Gran Turismo F07 is a five-door fastback produced from 2009 to 2017. It was BMW's first "Progressive Activity Sedan" (PAS) and sits between the 5 Series and 7 Series in terms of size and luxury. The F07 shares the F01 7 Series-derived platform and dashboard with the F10 5 Series. The engine range includes four- and six-cylinder diesels (N47, N57), a six-cylinder petrol (N55), and a V8 petrol (N63). A facelift in 2013 brought updated engines and revised styling. The F07 was succeeded by the 6 Series Gran Turismo G32 in 2017.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L6 (shared with F01 7 Series and F10 5 Series) • Predecessor: None (new model line) • Successor: BMW 6 Series Gran Turismo G32 • Sibling body styles in this generation: F10 Saloon, F11 Touring.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The F10/F07 family generated 300 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED], concentrated on the N57 diesel sixes and N63 V8s as these cars age out of warranty.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre straight-six diesel is the F07's most dependable engine — chain-driven, with a robust bottom end and proven reliability across the BMW range. 245–258 PS and 540 Nm of torque make it effortless, while the 8-speed automatic transmission complements it perfectly.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder diesel refinement without the 535d's complexity.

items.quote: The N55 is BMW's single-turbo straight-six that replaced the N54 — 306 PS, twin-scroll turbo, and significantly more reliable than its predecessor. The F07 was the first model to receive the N55. It's the petrol you can buy without HPFP anxiety.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder petrol performance without the N63's V8 running costs. (Model-wide: B58B30 in the G30 sets the modern benchmark — see G30 →)

items.quote: The hot-vee V8 suffers from heat-related failures — valve stem seals, turbo coolant lines, and battery drain are common. The N63 has a well-earned reputation for high ownership costs. The 550i is fast, but it's expensive to keep fast.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who want V8 performance and are prepared for the maintenance bills.

items.quote: Valve stem seal failure on the N63 requires significant disassembly — repair costs can reach £3,000–£6,000. A full engine replacement sits at £6,000–£10,000. This is the single most expensive ownership cost on the F07 range.
items.whoItsFor: 550i owners who budget for high-end maintenance.

items.quote: The 530d's 245 PS and 540 Nm offer effortless performance with 45+ mpg potential. The N57 is robust, the chain is accessible, and the F07's weight doesn't overwhelm it. It's the sweet spot of the diesel range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best balance of performance, economy, and reliability.

items.quote: The 520d is the entry-level F07 — 184 PS and 380 Nm from a 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel. It's the most economical engine in the range and the cheapest to replace. The N47's rear-chain risk is the caveat, but addressed proactively, it's a dependable workhorse.
items.whoItsFor: budget-conscious buyers who prioritise economy over performance.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — budget for chain history
Solid Choice — 258 PS, improved over pre-LCI
Performance Diesel — 313 PS, complex but rewarding

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve stem seals / turbo coolant lines
rows.ownershipVerdict: Most Expensive — 408–449 PS, high ownership costs

economicsRule.title: The F07 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,500–£7,000 N57 recon against a £6,000–£18,000 F07 sits at 25–117% of the car's value. On a sub-£8,000 F07, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £15,000+ low-mileage example, the same recon drops to a routine 30–47% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. The F07's unique fastback body means values can vary significantly — condition and specification matter more than age.

problems.title: N47 Timing Chain Stretch (520d)
problems.rootCause: The rear-mounted timing chain receives the last oil feed and is tensioned by a plastic guide that degrades over time — cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning. This affects the entire N47 family across the BMW range.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on any N47-powered F07, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if ignored. On an F07 worth under £8,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare N47 engine prices for your F07 520d →

problems.title: N63 Valve Stem Seal Failure (550i)
problems.rootCause: The N63's hot-vee design (turbochargers located in the V of the engine) creates significant heat — valve stem seals can harden and fail, leading to oil consumption, blue smoke on startup, and eventually, catalytic converter damage.
problems.recommendation: On any N63-powered F07, have the valve stem seals inspected at 50,000 miles — a £3,000–£6,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000–£10,000 engine replacement that follows if ignored. This is the defining ownership cost of the 550i.

cta.label: Compare N63 engine prices for your F07 550i →

problems.title: N57S Turbocharger Wear (535d)
problems.rootCause: The N57S in the 535d uses two turbochargers in a sequential arrangement — the smaller turbo can suffer from wear, leading to reduced boost, loss of power, and eventually, turbocharger failure. The N57S is otherwise a robust engine.
problems.recommendation: On any N57S-powered F07, have the turbochargers inspected at every major service — an £800–£2,000 turbo repair is far cheaper than the £3,500–£5,500 engine replacement that follows if ignored. The 535d is rewarding but requires proactive maintenance.

cta.label: Compare N57S turbocharger repair quotes for your F07 535d →

problems.title: Air Suspension Compressor Failure (rear air suspension)
problems.affectedModels: F07 models with optional rear air suspension (most UK-market examples with self-levelling).
problems.rootCause: The rear air suspension compressor can fail with age — the compressor motor seals deteriorate, leading to longer pump times, overheating, and eventual failure. The car may sit low at the rear or fail to level.
problems.recommendation: Have the air suspension system leak-tested at every service on F07s over 60,000 miles — a £500–£900 compressor repair is cheap insurance against the £1,200–£1,800 full system overhaul that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare F07 air suspension repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: F07 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are F07 Gran Turismo (fastback) only. F10 Saloon and F11 Touring share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages. The 520d was introduced at launch (2009) and continued through to 2017. The 530d was available from 2010 to 2017. The 535d was introduced in 2010. The 535i was available from launch. The 550i was available from launch with the N63B44, and the N63B44B replaced it for LCI models. xDrive (all-wheel drive) was available as an option on most variants.
